reviewed.by: TALK-OVER single-card reel on undesigned coincidences. 2026-07-13 REVISION per user: punchier WOW intro + REPLACED Herod's-court example with the Damascus-basket escape (Acts 9:23-25 + 2 Corinthians 11:32-33), a robust Acts-vs-letter interlock (immune to the Synoptic-source objection). RE-GATED CLEAN: citations 0 errors (both new passages verbatim vs ESV; fixed 'over the wall'->'down the wall' per the texts' 'through an opening in the wall'); orthodoxy CLEAN ('his enemies' avoids anti-Jewish pull-quote; scope bound intact); argument SOUND (0 BREAK; applied 2 WEAK fixes — reworded 'neither explains the other' to convey independence+mutual completion, and broadened the conclusion to 'the Gospels and Acts' since the 2nd example is Acts+a letter; tightened 'decades apart'->'independently'). Positive case only, NO steelman per the reel rule. Keeps the reliability-not-proof bound.

voiceover: There's a piece of evidence for the Gospels that most Christians have never heard of — and once you see it, you can't unsee it. Watch. When Jesus feeds five thousand people, John's Gospel says he turned to one disciple — Philip — and asked, 'Where are we to buy bread?' But why Philip? He wasn't the leader; that was Peter. He wasn't the treasurer; that was Judas. John raises the question and never answers it. Now here's the twist. Luke, telling the same story, drops in a detail John leaves out: it happened near a town called Bethsaida. And John — five chapters earlier, for a completely unrelated reason — mentions in passing that Philip was from Bethsaida. Put the three together and the puzzle dissolves: Jesus asked Philip because they were near Philip's hometown, and he'd know the local markets. But no single Gospel says that. You have to assemble it yourself, from three details scattered across different books. This is called an undesigned coincidence. And here's why it's so powerful: people who invent a story together agree on the big claims. What they almost never fake is a tiny detail in one book that quietly answers a riddle left open in another — with neither writer noticing. And it happens again and again. Take Paul's escape from Damascus. After he became a follower of Jesus, his enemies in the city wanted him dead. The book of Acts says they watched the city gates day and night, so his friends lowered him down the wall, at night, in a basket, to get him out. Years later, Paul himself — writing a letter — describes the very same escape: the same wall, the same basket. But he names a completely different watcher: the governor under King Aretas, guarding the city to seize him. One account names his enemies; the other names the governor's guard. Neither writer leaned on the other — but lock the two accounts together and the picture completes: private enemies couldn't seal every gate unless the governor's men were doing it with them. A whole city, watching for one man. Two writers, independently, remembering the same strange night. It doesn't prove the miracles happened. But it makes one thing very hard to believe — that the Gospels and Acts are late, made-up legend. They read like the residue of real memory of real events. Hidden in plain view.
